title: What do plants do with the glucose they make?
examTip: AQA often asks you to list uses of glucose in plants, or explain why glucose is stored as starch rather than as glucose. Make sure you can name all six uses and explain the nitrate ion connection to protein synthesis.
introTips: This lesson is about what a plant does with glucose once it has made it — there are six uses, and you need all of them.
You'll also look at why plants store glucose as starch and transport it as sucrose — and why these choices matter.
The nitrate ion link to protein synthesis is a favourite exam topic, so pay close attention to that section.
intro: A plant that photosynthesises all day is making glucose constantly — but it doesn't just sit on it. That glucose is the raw material for almost everything the plant does: growing, storing energy, building new structures, and making proteins. Understanding what the plant does with its glucose explains almost all of plant biology. It's also why farmers care so much about soil nutrients.

keyPoints.heading: Six things plants do with glucose
keyPoints.content: Plants use glucose in six main ways. Respiration: glucose is broken down to release energy for all cellular processes — this happens in every living cell, all the time. Cellulose: glucose is used to make cellulose for cell walls, providing structural support and keeping cells rigid. Starch: excess glucose is converted to starch for storage in roots, stems and leaves — starch is insoluble, which means it doesn't affect osmosis. Proteins: glucose (as a carbon skeleton) combined with nitrate ions from the soil is used to make amino acids, which are assembled into proteins for growth and enzymes. Fats and oils: glucose is converted to lipids for energy storage in seeds and for making cell membranes. Nectar: glucose is used to make sugary nectar to attract pollinators such as bees. All six are fair game for a 4- or 6-mark question.
writeBullets: Respiration — releases energy for all cell processes
Cellulose — builds cell walls for structural support
Starch — insoluble storage form in roots, stems, leaves
Proteins — glucose + nitrate ions → amino acids → proteins (growth and enzymes)
Fats and oils — energy storage in seeds; also used in cell membranes

keyTerms.def: A structural carbohydrate made from glucose that forms the rigid cell walls of plant cells
An insoluble storage carbohydrate made from glucose — stored in roots, stems and leaves
A sugary liquid produced by flowers to attract pollinators such as bees and butterflies

keyPoints.memTip: Remember all six with the phrase: Really Can't Stop Playing Football Nights — Respiration, Cellulose, Starch, Proteins, Fats/oils, Nectar. Six uses, six words. Say the phrase, then expand each one from memory.

keyPoints.heading: Why starch for storage, and sucrose for transport?
keyPoints.content: Glucose is soluble — if it built up in cells, it would lower the water potential and cause osmotic problems (water would rush in and cells would burst, or water distribution would be disrupted). So plants convert glucose into insoluble starch for storage. Starch has no osmotic effect because it doesn't dissolve. When the plant needs the energy later, starch is broken back down into glucose. For transport through the phloem, glucose is converted into sucrose — a disaccharide that is soluble but moves efficiently through sieve tubes to wherever it's needed. So the rule is: store as starch (insoluble, no osmotic effect), move as sucrose (soluble, efficiently transported).
writeBullets: Glucose is soluble — storing it would disrupt osmosis in cells
Converted to INSOLUBLE starch for storage → no osmotic effect
Starch → broken back down to glucose when energy is needed
Transported as SUCROSE (a disaccharide) through the phloem
Rule: store as starch, transport as sucrose

keyTerms.def: A disaccharide (glucose + fructose) used to transport sugars through the phloem in plants
The vascular tissue in plants that transports sugars (mainly sucrose) from leaves to the rest of the plant
The movement of water molecules from a region of high water potential to a region of lower water potential, through a partially permeable membrane
Does not dissolve in water — starch is insoluble, meaning it has no effect on the water potential of a cell

keyPoints.memTip: Ask yourself: why can't plants just store glucose as glucose? Because it would dissolve and mess with osmosis. Starch doesn't dissolve — so it's the safe storage form. Think of it like keeping cash in a safe (starch) rather than leaving it on the counter (glucose).

keyPoints.heading: Why nitrate ions matter for plant growth
keyPoints.content: Glucose provides carbon, hydrogen and oxygen — but proteins also need nitrogen. Plants get nitrogen in the form of nitrate ions (NO₃⁻) absorbed from the soil through their roots. Nitrate ions supply the nitrogen needed to build amino acids, which are then joined together to make proteins. Proteins are essential for growth (structural proteins) and for controlling reactions (enzymes). If a plant is nitrate deficient — for example in poor soil — it cannot make enough amino acids. The result is stunted growth and yellowing of the older leaves (chlorosis), because the plant breaks down chlorophyll from older leaves to recycle the nitrogen to where it's most needed.
writeBullets: Plants need nitrogen to make amino acids and proteins
Nitrogen is absorbed as nitrate ions (NO₃⁻) from the soil
Glucose + nitrate ions → amino acids → proteins
Nitrate deficiency → stunted growth (not enough protein for growth)
Nitrate deficiency → yellowing leaves (chlorosis) — older leaves affected first

keyTerms.def: Mineral ions (NO₃⁻) absorbed by plant roots from the soil — provide the nitrogen needed to make amino acids and proteins
The building blocks of proteins — made from glucose combined with nitrogen from nitrate ions
The yellowing of plant leaves caused by a lack of chlorophyll — often a symptom of nitrate or magnesium deficiency
A lack of nitrate ions in the soil, leading to stunted growth and yellowing leaves in plants

keyPoints.memTip: Nitrogen comes from NO₃⁻ in the soil. No nitrogen → no amino acids → no proteins → no growth. The yellowing starts in older leaves because the plant cannibalises its own chlorophyll, recycling nitrogen from leaves it no longer needs to keep its youngest growth alive.

1.question: Why do plants store glucose as starch rather than keeping it as glucose?
options: Starch contains more energy per molecule than glucose
Glucose is toxic to plant cells at high concentrations
Starch is insoluble so it doesn't affect the water potential of the cell
Starch is easier to transport through the phloem
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Starch is insoluble, so it doesn't dissolve and doesn't change the water potential of the cell. That means it has no osmotic effect — the plant can store as much as it likes without disrupting water balance.
1.wrongFeedback: The key word is 'insoluble'. Glucose dissolves in the cytoplasm and would alter the water potential of the cell, causing osmotic problems. Starch doesn't dissolve, so it can be stored without affecting water movement. Starch is also not transported through the phloem — sucrose is.

2.question: A farmer notices that her crop plants have stunted growth and yellowing older leaves. Which mineral ion is most likely deficient in the soil?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — nitrate deficiency causes stunted growth (not enough nitrogen to make proteins) and yellowing of older leaves as the plant recycles nitrogen from them to support new growth.
2.wrongFeedback: Nitrate ions provide nitrogen for making amino acids and proteins. Without nitrates, the plant can't grow properly and breaks down chlorophyll in older leaves to recycle the nitrogen — causing them to yellow. Magnesium deficiency also causes yellowing, but it tends to affect younger leaves and is linked to chlorophyll production directly.

commonMistakes: Only listing three or four uses of glucose and missing starch, fats/oils or nectar — AQA often awards one mark per use, so you lose marks for every one you forget.
Saying plants store glucose as starch 'to save energy' — the real reason is osmotic: starch is insoluble and doesn't affect water potential. Always use the word 'insoluble'.
Confusing phloem transport with xylem transport — sugars (sucrose) are transported in the phloem; water and minerals travel in the xylem. Don't mix these up.
Forgetting that nitrate ions provide nitrogen, not glucose — glucose provides carbon, hydrogen and oxygen; the nitrogen for amino acids comes separately from NO₃⁻ absorbed by roots.
examTips: If asked for 'uses of glucose in plants', write a numbered list and aim for all six — the mark scheme often awards one mark per correct use, so more is better.
When explaining starch storage, always include the word 'insoluble' and link it to osmosis — 'starch is insoluble so it does not affect the water potential of the cell' is the model answer phrase.
For nitrate deficiency questions, give both symptoms: stunted growth AND yellowing of older leaves — each symptom may carry a separate mark.

revisionCardBullets: Six uses of glucose: Respiration, Cellulose (cell walls), Starch (storage), Proteins (growth/enzymes), Fats and oils (seed energy store), Nectar (pollinators)
Mnemonic: Really Can't Stop Playing Football Nights
Starch = INSOLUBLE storage → no osmotic effect on the cell
Transported as SUCROSE through the phloem (not as glucose)
Store as starch, transport as sucrose
Nitrogen for proteins comes from NITRATE IONS (NO₃⁻) absorbed from soil
Glucose + nitrate ions → amino acids → proteins
Nitrate deficiency → stunted growth + yellowing of OLDER leaves (chlorosis)
